Business Lookahead: Rate decisions, business activity surveys STORY: From central bank meetings to reports from some of the "Magnificent Seven," these are the stories to watch in business and finance in the coming week.:: Look Ahead:: Set to pauseThe European Central Bank is set to pause on Thursday after eight consecutive rate cuts halved its policy rate to 2%.It comes as a 30% U.S. tariff threat looms over the euro zone.Policymakers will be reluctant to create the sense that they are reacting to a threat, but they’ll have to reassess their worst case scenario from June, which foresaw a lower tariff level.:: Earnings in earnestMore than one-fifth of S&P 500 companies are expected to report in the coming week.Market heavyweights Alphabet and Tesla are the first of the “Magnificent Seven” to report this period.:: Coca-ColaResults are also due from Coca-Cola, IBM and Phillip Moris International.:: Business timeJuly surveys of business activity across the globe may capture shifts in behavior in both services and manufacturing in response to Trump’s new August 1 tariff deadline.Twenty-two of the world’s 34 largest economies have slowing activity, leaving the services sector to do much of the heavy lifting.In June, services activity in the U.S., euro zone, China and Germany were slower than in December, and well below last June.Among richer nations, only Japan and Britain saw a year-on-year improvement in service-sector activity last month.:: Turkey’s central bankTurkey’s central bank is expected to return to rate cuts on Thursday.That's after the March detention of Istanbul Mayor Ekrem Imamoglu roiled markets, knocked the lira to a record low, saw stocks trading suspended and prompted the central bank to hike overnight rates to 46%.A Reuters poll showed expectations for a 250-basis point cut, with some predicting as much as 350 bps.Central banks in Hungary and Russia also hold rate meetings in the coming week.

Coca-Cola to roll out cane sugar coke, which Trump says is his doing STORY: Donald Trump says it’s his doing.Coca-Cola confirmed Tuesday (July 22) that it plans to roll out a version of Coke sweetened with cane sugar for the U.S. market.The news comes just a week after Trump posted on social media that he had pushed for the change.“This will be a very good move by them”, he said, adding “you’ll see, it’s just better!”.Coke in the U.S. has been sweetened with corn syrup, though some other markets do use cane sugar.Experts say there’s no major difference between the two with regards to health, saying too much of either is bad for you.Now Coca-Cola says the new version will complement existing products.But it’s likely to drive up costs for the firm, with cane sugar more expensive than corn syrup.U.S. corn refiners have also complained that a switch will hurt demand for their product, and lead to more imports of sugar.Coca-Cola is also battling the impact of Trump’s trade wars.It has said it’s looking at using more plastic bottles, with metal for cans facing a 50% import tariff.The firm has also faced a boycott from Hispanic customers in the U.S. and Mexico, after a viral video showed the firm laying off Latino workers and reporting them to immigration officials.Reuters in February found no public evidence that the company had reported its migrant employees to ICE.Despite all that, Coca-Cola saw revenue rise 2.5% to just over $12.6 billion for the latest quarter, beating forecasts.Though the volume of drinks sold declined over the period, that was more than offset by a rise in prices.
